title: Delegated legislation
summary: 9084 — types, reasons for, and controls on delegated legislation.
learningObjectives: Define delegated legislation and its types.
Explain why Parliament delegates law-making power.

simpleExplanation.title: Law made outside Parliament
simpleExplanation.summary: Parliament grants ministers and bodies power to make detailed rules — faster and more flexible, but raises democratic accountability concerns.
steps: Types — Orders in Council, statutory instruments, bylaws.
Reasons — time, detail, local knowledge, emergencies.

flashcards.back: Most common DL — government ministers under enabling Act.
Made by Privy Council — emergencies, transfer of powers.
Local authorities/public bodies — e.g. parking restrictions.
SI becomes law unless rejected within 40 days.
Must be actively approved by Parliament before in force.
Beyond powers granted — courts can declare DL invalid.

sections.content: **Delegated legislation** (1.1.3) — know **three types**, **controls** (parliamentary + judicial), and **procedural/substantive ultra vires**.

sections.question: Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of delegated legislation. [15 marks]
sections.solution:
**Advantages:** Saves **Parliamentary time**; **technical detail** by experts; **flexibility** to amend quickly; **local bylaws** suit local needs; **emergency** Orders in Council (e.g. foot-and-mouth).

**Disadvantages:** **Undemocratic** — unelected ministers; **lack of scrutiny** — many SIs pass unnoticed (negative resolution); **volume** hard to monitor; **sub-delegation** concerns.

**Controls:** **Parliament** — committees (Scrutiny Committee), affirmative/negative procedures; **Courts** — *Agricultural Fisheries case* (procedural ultra vires), *Attorney-General v Fulham* (substantive).

**Conclusion:** **Necessary and efficient** but requires **strong controls** to maintain accountability.
